sentences.text: I remember walking in the camp.
And what I thought-- in her misery, she was pretty good-looking.
And I said, can I help you?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
She says, why did you liberate us?
I said, what are you talking about?
Under the Nazis, I knew I was going to die.
If it's not today, it's tomorrow, the following week.
I knew we were going to die.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You've liberated us.
I know I've got to live.
And I have nothing to live for.
I'm nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that split second, I didn't know what to say.
So I moved further into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wait a minute.
Wait a minute.
So I walked back to her.
What do you want?
Send John Steinbeck here.
And again, at that moment, I couldn't think who John Steinbeck was.
Then it comes back to me.
And this helped me to convince myself and other people that they-- the Nazis hadn't destroyed the, the intellect and the feelings of these people.
They tried to animalize them, brutalize them, make them feel that they're vermin.
But when she said, bring us-- bring me John Steinbeck.
I said, why John Steinbeck?
I said, you'll be able to write what you see with your eyes.
He'll be able to write and describe what went through our minds every second of the day under these beasts.
And that helped tremendously, tremendously.
That helped you?
It helped me.
I hope it helped her.
I said, certainly we're, we're going to-- I assured them of everything.
I assure them that they were beautiful, although their hair was-- they were shorn.
Their hairs were shorn, just beginning to grow again-- told them that they were beautiful and everything.
So-- a woman likes to be schmoozed, if you like, and told that she's beautiful.
And that was my gift, apparently.
That's why they think now I'm a-- what do you call it?
A womanizer.
And, and so we went round and round.
Oh yes, then we went-- then Eva Singer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she-- as the-- that's before I came in.
The tanks came in.
And she was looking out from the hut, and she saw the British tanks.
She gave a yell and-- of happiness.
She broke rank, and she rushed forward.
The Nazis had not yet been disarmed.
So automatically, he drew his gun to shoot.
Then this Derrek Sington drew his revolver very quickly and said, drop yours, or I'll drop you.
That's how she was saved.
I got to know of her.
She became a teacher of the kids there.
And she spoke to me.
I gave her chocolates, the very thing-- the worst thing-- where a woman, she's been shorn of love and affection to give her sweets things.
And I said-- she fell in love.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, I didn't realize it too much.
She said, I, I, I was engaged-- a medical student.
Well, tell me his name.
We found out he was in Buchenwald.
We brought him back into Belsen.
He had a face and a body only a mother could love, and forgive me, oh God.
And she had to be blind-- terrible state.
Obviously, there was no love of-- feeling of love for him.
I said, Eva give him a chance.
Give him a chance.
We'll build him up like we're building you up and the others.
Look, I've got to go out.
The relief teams are coming.
I've got to go out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, please God, all well, and you decide to get married, I'll-- if I'm in Germany, I'll come and marry you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I didn't go out as quickly as I thought.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, look, I'm married.
I've got kids.
What do you want?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I went home.
Home to England or--
England--
--or to--
--to Leeds.
I was on my leave.
They-- the, the army almost forced me to go out.
Three and a half months.
Oh, that-- I was-- oh, that-- terrible business, terrible.
They thought I was going mental.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think-- I was glad to get out, to be honest as well.
So I went home.
And I couldn't sleep at home-- lecturing here, lecturing, lecturing, lecturing.
And there was a big meeting in Leeds.
And my colleague, who's now a rabbi in Jerusalem, retired, of course, with his wife-- he had no children.
I said, [?
Isaac and ?] [?
Fanny, ?] come and have supper with us.
She said, look, Leslie, I've just-- after your lecture, I can't use the material I've just bought.
I've bought a piece of cloth for a dress.
It's a negligee and silk stockings and blah, blah, blah.
I said, to how many people can I send it?
What do you mean how many?
I said, well, one stocking to one girl and another stocking to another.
That's how-- we played God.
And we, we never had sufficient things to go round at the early-- the early stages.
Don't be silly.
Send it.
Who did I send-- I sent it to Eva by post.
When I came back, [MOAN] Yossele Rosensaft, the chairman of the Jewish committee then-- what have you done to Eva?
What do you mean, what have I done to her?
You sent her all these beautiful things.
It means you want to marry her.
And she says goodbye to [NON-ENGLISH], her fiance-- oy vey.
And I shook her by the shoulders.
I said, I promise you, he looked much better at that time.
Soon as you say-- give me the word, I'll come and marry you.
Six, seven months go by.
She telephones, and I went to marry her.
Beautiful hall there and about 200 people-- they were all guests, no invitation.
They all-- they all lit a candle.
All carried a candle.
It was a lovely sight.
And after the chuppah, I broke the glass, shouting, mazel tov!
She says, how do I look?
I say, look, you look-- you look very beautiful.
Don't you recognize it?
And what do you mean, recognize it?
That's the-- I made a dress out of the material, and these are my stockings, and so on.
And so that was a nice thing.
Now, when I spoke to [?
Esther ?] [?
Anderson ?] about this, she contacted the, the, the press.
She contacted various people in Israel to find out-- to bring Eva over.
By the time she found her, it was just about too late.
But anyhow, she couldn't have-- she wouldn't have come because her husband was very ill.
And she didn't want to leave him.
So that's the story of Eva Singer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They stayed--
I went back to Belsen, not for-- just for only a few days.
And then all the relief teams had come again.
Everything is getting shipshape.
And I went back to Plon, the Plon on the-- on the-- on the Baltic.
There, we find there was a, a U-boat base there.
Then we found a large number of bodies floating.
It appears that the enemy was taking many, many Jews further into the field, and the-- further into Germany, or wherever.
And the, the RAF was flying over.
They saw a German boat with people.
They couldn't recognize if they were refugees or German soldiers.
And they let fly-- and the bodies floating in the-- in the waters there.
So they said it was too difficult a task to bring them out and bury them.
So they shot them dead.
They peppered them with bullets.
And the, the, the, the bodies sank.
That was also another unpleasant thing.
Oh yes, and three, three of them were saved.
Not the dead bodies of, of that group down there-- a mother and two daughters, which was a miracle of miracles.
How the Nazis allowed the three of them to live-- they usually tortured them by shooting one, or shooting two.
The mother and the, the, the elder daughter could speak English.
So the colonel had the mother.
And the, the, the other officer-- how-- well, what about the younger girl?
She can't speak English.
I can't let her go back to the camps.
And then, poor me, I had to take them.
And then a parcel, I forget-- well, she helped.
It was doing a rachmones.
It was mercy rather than efficiency that I wanted.
A parcel came for me.
It was soap.
So I offered her a bar of soap.
She jumped as if she was electrocuted.
I've forgotten her name now.
What's the name?
What's, what's the trouble?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She says, my father's missing.
And so far as I know, many of the Jews were killed.
And their fat was made into soap.
So it took me minutes to convince her this soap is from England.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was coming back, and I-- out of my office, I noticed a lieutenant and a sergeant or a corporal.
I went-- why can't I remember her name?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I say, what?
Who, who are they?
What are they?
She said, they're looking for evidence either to acquit or to punish the commandant in the camp where we were liberated.
I gave evidence in favor of the commandant.
Why?
If it wasn't for him, many of us would have been killed by the-- by the SS inside.
He was a, a businessman in the First World War.
Had very, very many friends, Jewish friends.
He was too old to, to join the army.
So they gave him this job.
But he couldn't bring himself to murder as the others did.
So from that point of view, I, I gave evidence in his favor.
What happened, I don't know.
Then she told me that six girls, who, as they were being marched through the streets going to further-- to the camps, or wherever it was, the civilians used to shoot from their windows.
Six girls were killed.
And I believe they were buried in the-- in the cemetery.
I said, come on, show me the cemetery.
And at that particular time, the order came-- no British soldier should frat with a German girl.
The news of the-- of the-- of the horrors of these camps was spread amongst the troops.
And the order was no-- don't frat with these German girls.
So as I was walking with her in the street to the cemetery, everybody shouted, don't frat, don't frat!
She said, what are they saying?
I explained to her.
She said, I better put a card in front of me that I'm a Jewish girl.
I'm not a German.
I said, never mind.
We went into the cemetery.
My batman driver was with me and his gun.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't put it in a question, interrogatively.
I said, where's the burial plot, where the, the six girls were buried?
And my driver was clicking his gun.
The fellow was probably nervous-- superintendent, whatever they called them there.
And he showed me.
It was flat as this table, flat.
You couldn't tell it was a grave.
I said to myself, I better report it, whether we could intern.
But it was impossible to-- who had the time or the energy?
People weren't worried where they should re-inter the bodies.
So they're probably still there.
And then, then we visited troops here, visited troops there.
And there's another lovely story.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was another camp, a smallish camp.
And one woman said, I dreamt I went to my sister in America.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she brought me to her house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was fiddling with something in the door.
She said, what are you looking for?
She said, I'm looking for the string to open the door.
She was thinking of which she was living in a tent, and, you know, and then these pieces of string would fasten the tent.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the sister nodded.
And she opened the door, and she went in.
And she'd sit down-- it's a beautiful settee.
Oh, it's a shame to spoil the settee.
I'll sit on the floor.
Then she gave them coffee.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she only drank half.
What's wrong with it?
Oh, I can use that for a shampoo.
I can wash my head.
You know, the whole things with-- how they lived in the camp was working through their minds.
And, of course, some of them needed psychiatric treatment eventually.
Right, so then I married-- I married two, two, two, two, two.
And then Norman and Gina Turgel I married.
We tried to marry them in Belsen.
But the order came for his unit to move.
They went to Schleswig-Holstein-- in Lubeck.
So I managed to get over there.
Oh yes, he was one of the officers there who arrested Kramer and all the other beasts.
And, and there you are.
She was very lucky.
He said he fell in love with her straightaway.
But when I married them, I was terrified that she had an elderly mother.
And usually, in-- even in any country under normal circumstances, a young bridegroom doesn't want a mother-in-law to be foisted on him right from the very beginning.
And I was worried.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But as soon as I married them, I left.
I didn't want to think of it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was 1945.
In 1947, I was appointed as the minister of Hendon.
And who-- and lo and behold, who among the members were Mr. and Mrs.
Turgel and the old lady.
That gave me great pleasure.
And she lived to 99.
She wanted always to go to Israel.
But she couldn't do it.
We had to take her body there.
She, she wanted to be buried there.
She lost six children, I think.
And that's the story.
What happened to--
--since the war?
Well, we came home.
We came home.
And then I went back to Leeds.
My job was open.
But lo and behold, I found the street in which the synagogue was situated had been put out of bounds to American troops.
I was quite naive.
I didn't quite understand what it meant.
And then they told me what it meant.
There was brothels there.
I said, I don't want to-- I want to go to a shul in that-- in that area.
So I applied.
Finchley was vacant.
Hendon was vacant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sheffield was vacant.
So I applied to three of them.
Josi, bless her, wanted to come to London.
But she stopped me going, going on with Finchley because her dear school friend of hers was the minister there-- only temporary.
Because all those who had temporary jobs were told, you can't hold a job permanently.
However brilliant and good you are, it's got to be open.
Or the old minister's got to be taken back.
And he wanted to stay there.
She said, you're not-- so I, I, I withdrew.
They came to see me.
The [INAUDIBLE] officers beg of me to reapply-- no.
Sheffield wanted me, so I preached at Sheffield.
I preached at Hendon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether I dare say this.
Well, they're all dead now, anyhow.
So I'm not, not libeling them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a number of the people of Hendon wanted me.
Obviously, there were scores of colleagues who wanted the job.
And there was a number of balebatim, worthy balebatim, intelligent balebatim, wanted somebody else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had the Rabbi Cohen of Sheffield, of blessed memory, as a relation of these balebatim who wanted somebody else in Hendon-- told him, get Hardman appointed quickly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, this sort of maneuver was known to Mr.
Kosman, ?] alav hashalom, the warden of Hendon, who wanted me.
And he said, I'm hearing rumors that you're going to Sheffield.
I said, Mr.
Kosman, ?] do me a favor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm working for Hendon.
Unless you hear from my mouth-- not even a recording, my mouth-- don't believe anything you read or hear.
But the is like the Bible to some people.
It's written in the
I arrived-- the week before, in Sheffield, I spoke.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said I was ready, under certain conditions, to accept Sheffield because the Hendon job had not come up yet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And apparently-- this is Monday night.
The people in Sheffield were told by certain people in Hendon, get it through the It should appear on Friday morning that Hardman has got the job at Sheffield.
Mr.
Kosman-- ?] see, in Leeds, we don't get the before Saturday morning.
In London, you get it Friday morning.
And when he reads this, he goes, mashugana.
He phones me up 9 o'clock Friday morning.
I said, Joe, do me a favor.
I've told you.
I don't know what's cooking around here, but lest you hear it from me.
Right, then, in that case, send a disclaimer.
I immediately wrote a letter to the editor.
He phones me up.
I'm not publishing it.
Why?
For your sake.
You're in the middle of a very tough crowd here.
And you're a young man.
I don't want you to go down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
If I publish it, and then Hendon-- that you don't want Sheffield, and Hendon falls through, what kind of a life are you going to have in Sheffield?
You'll be at the beck and call of every [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Thank you very much, sir.
First time an editor, so far as I know, has saved the life of a young minister.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Right, so two or three people's names were put forward, one special.
And there's a mass meeting, a congregation meeting.
And they voted against it-- voted against it.
They were shocked and stunned.
What's going to happen?
So Waley Cohen-- Sir Robert Waley Cohen was the president of the United Synagogue.
And he, he chaired that meeting.
And he said, well, we'll, we'll, we'll open it up again.
So somebody said, no.
We've got-- the cream of applicants applied for this job.
And I can assure you, he said, nobody's-- will apply again.
In that case, who's the next one on the list?
Hardman.
Right, all hands up for Hardman?
And they all went up for Hardman.
And when I heard about this, the men-- all this was told to me afterwards.
When I heard about it, my first sermon [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was to tell them we need unity now.
I'm here.
I'll do my best.
There's nothing worse than a young man being tortured or digged at, trying to make a go of his new position.
So that's it.
That it-- whatever.
Then I think my Aviva was born, aleha hashalom.
And then went, went, went-- life went on, went, went on, went on, went on.
But you have given talks and told people about your experiences--
Oh, yes--
Oh yes-- and, yes, well, he was invited.
You were invited all over the [NON-ENGLISH] to give talks.
And I used to preach about it almost every other Shabbat.
And one dear friend of mine--
Almost right from the beginning.
There's always something to do with the Holocaust, always.
People got fed up.
And one man said, Leslie, haven't you got anything else?
[NON-ENGLISH], enough.
I said, you're quite right.
So I stopped.
And suddenly, the Holocaust came up again in the public eye.
I said, in that case, I'll start again.
And there you are.
Oh, then, one of the-- in connection-- when the letters came through from the world to these people--
Back into Belsen.
The officer commanding said, get them to line up.
And we'll shout out the name, and they'll come and collect.
I said, no sir, forgive me.
That reminds me again they're under the Nazis.
They've all got their own, own rooms or dormitories, whatever it is.
I'll appoint a postman.
And he'll deliver it to them.
It's all part of the rehabilitation.
I use common sense to the nth degree because I, I, I wasn't a qualified psychologist, or whatever it is, or social worker.
Oh, how much-- I didn't believe it.
That's why I'm not too happy with certain social workers here.
The darn trouble is that if you're not qualified yourself, then they can pooh-pooh you, however right you might be.
However, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go and find a postman.
[LAUGHS] The order was, none of these people were allowed on the German streets.
We were terrified in case they'd commit murder.
I was going out to my billet, and a young man comes in.
I said, where have you been?
And we spoke in Yiddish.
He says, I've been out in the German street the first time in five years.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SPEAKING YIDDISH] I said, what do you mean?
It means, I couldn't kill them.
Oh my God, what's happened?
I said, what are you talking about?
Haven't you heard of the gas chambers?
I said, I have.
Have you heard of the crematoria?
I said, I have.
They murdered us.
They murdered my own parents in front of me.
And people, kids, I saw in the street, young men and women-- they kicked us.
They spat on us.
And I couldn't kill them.
Then he had-- I said, has he killed them?
How do I know?
Then the magic words that I've been waiting for-- because the officers kept saying to me, Padre, what are you crying for?
What you're doing, they're not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they may not be worth keeping alive.
How can they be normal after going through this hell of hells?
I said, give them a chance.
Give them a chance, as I believe the Jew is born civilized.
That no matter they tried to destroy us, we-- as long as we're on our feet, we'll, we'll jump.
We'll come back again into our own.
So I rushed to the major.
And I said, I've got my postman.
And I told him this.
So I go to the unit and the tailor there, whatever it is.
And I give him a, a battle dress.
I think it was too, too big for him.
But he wouldn't let me take it off his shoulders-- nah.
So he went around, got it sent out.
Then I said to them, do you-- have got anybody in the-- in the world that you could-- he said, yeah, I've got an uncle or somebody in England.
Later, I found out he was one of the general secretaries of the United Synagogue.
So I wrote.

I said, I don't know who you are, whether you're rich or whether you're poor, whether you're religious, whether you're not, whether you do mitzvahs day after day, night after night, save this young man!
And you'll get Gan Eden in 120 years.
That's how I used to write.
That's how I used to write.
He took him over.
This is '45.
In '47, '48, I was already in Hendon.
And we had a chuppah.
And who was one of the-- who was the hazzan under that chuppah?
This little fellow-- unbelievable, unbelievable.
And then-- but I believe later on he divorced his wife.
She divorced him.
What else?
What else?
Then we went to-- and we lived-- we lived in Station Road, talked [NON-ENGLISH], bar mitzvahs.
We had, oh, a tremendous number of bar mitzvahs.
The shuls would be packed, packed.
We had a wonderful hazzan.
That may be one of the reasons.
But in my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] selfishness or self-opinionatedness, I hoped that, [LAUGHS] that they came to hear me.
But anyhow, it was packed.
We used to bring in extra chairs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a-- it was a wonderful-- it was a wonderful life.
It didn't pay very well.
But, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but there you are.
That's how it goes.
And then, after 32 years, I retired.
And all my children were married.
And then the great blow is when my eldest daughter went on Aliyah.
And then the, the youngest one, she also wanted to go.
So they decided-- there's a group of them going.
They'd rather tour the country for awhile for a, a week before we settle down.
And then there was a-- they went-- they ordered a car from here or from there.
There must have been a tire burst.
And they went over a ravine.
And they were all injured, but she was the only one that was killed.
[CRYING]
She was 21, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes.
And then my third daughter was pregnant at the time.
And she loved her sister, that [INAUDIBLE].
That trouble-- that was trouble, then.
Her husband, instead of being a decent fellow, realizing that she wasn't ready for sex during the week of shivah, and so on, he goes out with another woman.
So eventually-- she eventually divorced him.
And there you are.
And that's it.
My life story.
And so I've been 60 years-- 50 years in, in Hendon, this next year, which is '97.
And, please God, I will be celebrating our diamond wedding.
October, 1966.
1996-- that's it.
Oh yes.
Then suddenly, I get a-- about what?
Oh yes, all right, afterwards.
